title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, and directed staff to develop $100/day fines for illegal sewer discharge violations. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and inspection services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer extension agreement for Radkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project now likely moving to 2028. The grant covers about 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered ongoing repairs on Weston Avenue by Integrity Grading and Excavating to address pavement settling over sewer trenches, and upcoming repairs on Scoffield Avenue and Fuller Street for manhole and concrete issues.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the water system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Staff identified sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers at locations including the Kathleen lift station. An INI study with in-pipe technologies is planned to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million due to well 7 and 8 additions and new water main installations. The utility serves 6,100 customers with 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution seeks improved state transportation funding for local road maintenance and operations beyond one-time grants. He noted Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Michael presented the recycling program at Ryan Street site, with King Gravel submitting the low bid at $4.23/ton versus $4.25 from competitor. Estimated 30,000 tons equals $126,900. Michael demonstrated savings of approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, totaling about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of illegal clear water discharge into sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee members Zagami and Jordan distinguished between unknowing versus willful violations. Committee directed staff to develop enforcement language starting at $100/day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Extension Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael proposed allowing a duplex builder at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral until Radkey Avenue reconstruction when mains would be extended. Committee directed adding language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously with the date amendment.

Committee approved agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road reconstruction, with Weston responsible for water and sewer costs estimated at $94,860. Rothschild holds the contract with Steam Construction. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project: MSA at $37,315, Clark Dietz at $48,910 - MSA recommended. For Bladel Avenue: MSA at $70,400, Clark Dietz at $73,730 - Clark Dietz recommended due to having full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding forwarded to village board
King Gravel awarded 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated)
Staff directed to develop $100/day fine language for illegal sanitary sewer discharge violations
Radkey Avenue temporary water/sewer connection agreement to include 2029 reconstruction deadline
Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild approved at estimated $94,860 for Weston utilities
MSA approved for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction inspection ($37,315)
Clark Dietz approved for Bladel Avenue construction inspection ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue scheduled May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact at next meeting
Norcon to begin Scoffield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing balloon event in Marshfield has been canceled, potentially boosting attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work Product Transmittals and Educational Presentations
New Business - Grant Request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from Staff and Commission Members

discussions.body: The commission elected officers for the reorganized committee. Trustee Erling was nominated for Chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair by motion, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as Secretary.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion carried with a second by Renee, passing unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event has been canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned room night tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant (same as last year), with a request that organizers provide better hotel room data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously with Jackson seconding.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, describing how the candidates toured Marathon County attractions including Mullen's Cheese. She noted the winner was Anastasia, and that this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The candidates expressed appreciation for the community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all commissioners present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property. Chief Jeremy explained the building is designed for future growth, with current capacity for 40 officers but room for 50-60 in the future. Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting it be positioned further north to allow for future expansion. The landscaping plan meets current relaxed code requirements. Motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously with conditions 2-5 as listed in staff report.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and site drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring that facility into compliance. Motion to approve the program passed unanimously, with an amendment to report back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee.
Motion to approve minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email notification and calendar invites.

Electric vehicle charging stations added for future discussion, likely in the fall. Commissioner asked about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing. Staff noted Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications which could be shared with commissioners and potentially incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke remotely. He asked the commission to use common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous relaxed standards for Green Heck Turner. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on inspection results, noting some zoning complaints have gone unresolved for years.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with two annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings back to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg well regarding wellhead protection compliance
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations to be discussed at future meeting in fall
Ehlers TIF/workforce housing presentation to potentially be shared with commissioners

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on beyond approving previous meeting minutes. The meeting provided comprehensive overviews of departmental missions, budgets, and current initiatives.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (remote participation noted)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Services Department presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting aye.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting against environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates at age two, public health vending machines offering free resources including tick kits, and the new homelessness system response position. The department has 37.6 FTE staff across environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administrative teams. Administrator Leonard noted about half the $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant intergovernmental grants and public charges for services.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, describing the department's nearly 140 FTE staff working in child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing housing instability among child welfare and youth justice populations. The 2026 budget shows over $22 million total with approximately $7 million from tax levy. Leonard indicated potential restructuring requests coming due to increased need in areas like children's long-term support.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his office is now at 3.0 FTE after board approval, with customer interactions jumping from 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. He highlighted the PACT Act's impact on presumptive claims, $44 million in annual compensation coming to county veterans, and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans). Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo confirmed state statute requires each county to have a VSO and explained good working relationships with neighboring counties.

Executive Director Mike Ray described the four-county organization (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) serving as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services. About 11% of budget is county levy. He highlighted challenges with the nutrition/Meals on Wheels program, noting 40-45% increase in food costs over four years with flat federal funding. The Older Americans Act hasn't been reauthorized in over two years. The organization has about 60 FTE plus 385-400 volunteers. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meal program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: Schedule North Central Healthcare for separate full presentation before committee
Next meeting corrected to Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as originally stated)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggestions for future agenda items
Strategic plan review to be presented at upcoming meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bicycle regulations, and discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made committee appointments.

agenda.item: Board of Review adjournment to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order and roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bicycles
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks real data on park maintenance costs to justify the fees. Trustee Motti supported the increase, stating it's fair for new homes to pay for increased park needs. Ordinance 26-010 passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was moved by Hermling, seconded by Clark, and passed unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates a regional inspection agreement between Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside. Under this system, if any participating department inspects a food truck and applies a sticker, other departments will accept that inspection without requiring their own. This saves time and money for food truck operators. The ordinance was moved by Lopesell, seconded by Zagami, and passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. Discussion centered on removing specific language about health, safety, and welfare requirements for remote attendance. Trustee Beck raised concerns that the policy language didn't match what was discussed at the previous meeting. The board voted to strike sentences requiring justification based on health, safety, or welfare. The amendment passed with two no votes from Hermling and Motti. The final amended policy passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, seconded by Lopesell, passed unanimously. The board discussed having committee applications shared with board members in future packets. Openings remain on parks, finance/HR (two), and CLIPS (one).

The board discussed concerns about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue location when the property owner decided to develop it. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass near Alderson Street. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the old location to 53 spots currently, and vendors now can keep their vehicles with them. Mont Miller Park was considered but rejected due to its remote location. The board acknowledged this is discussion only with no action taken, noting if no suitable location is found, the market may need to suspend operations.

discussions.item: Engineering Tech Position and Outside Contractors
discussions.body: During public comment, Jim Pitel criticized the village for spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants instead of filling the vacant engineering technician position. Michael from Public Works explained the position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skill set is difficult to find locally, noting even consulting firms are pulling staff from southeast Wisconsin due to workforce shortages in central Wisconsin.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5201 Schofield Avenue spoke during public comment criticizing the village for spending $108,000 on outside engineering contractors instead of filling the vacant engineering technician position, questioning why Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being officially appointed by the board, and criticizing the lack of detailed Kennedy Park information in the meeting packet. He also commented during the park impact fee public hearing that the village lacks real cost data to justify the fee increases.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor license ordinance 26-008 approved creating regional inspection reciprocity
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand article dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Speed study on Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to the board
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6 PM
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for building inspection assistance to Ringle Township

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted Ringle and other communities. The board also approved consent agenda items and budget resolutions related to out-of-home placement and social services.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments discussion and remarks from Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
Vote on landfill tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks thanking Vice Chair Boots for assistance in developing committee appointments, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspective, developing new supervisors as leaders, and respecting supervisor preferences. He noted over 34% of the board is in its first or second term. The motion to approve committee appointments by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large Executive Committee positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, noting she advocates for the county's smallest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were used for voting. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
discussions.body: County Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted Ringle and other communities. The state is waiving its $13.10 fee, Ringle is waiving its $2.33 fee, and the county proposed reducing fees to approximately $36.94 per ton (commercial rate minus state and local fees) to cover operational costs. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county wave fees entirely for affected residents. Administrator Leonard mentioned exploring a partnership with the community foundation to help reimburse unreimbursed costs. The board voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.
Items C10 through C14A were approved unanimously on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck.
Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested the county wave fees entirely for affected residents, noting private businesses have donated significant resources (one up to $40,000) and requesting a six-month waiver period. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners whose houses must be demolished, noting some residents are still waiting for insurance adjusters and requesting extended fee relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved to reduce landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris through June 17th
Administrator to work with community foundation on mechanism to reimburse unreimbursed debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Board to revisit tipping fee relief at May meeting if extension beyond state declaration period is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to recommend a limited fee waiver for tornado-damaged properties to the full county board. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. CPZ staff recommended approval finding the rezoning consistent with the town's comprehensive plan. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Lever asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained there is currently a home and horse riding stable there, and the rezoning would make all structures conforming uses rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Miskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17th tornado affecting towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future incidents. Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for pouts systems, excluding other zoning and septic fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution to forward only reconnect fee waivers to the county board was then approved, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher provided an overview of department operations including the landfill facility, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment requirements by 2032 due to PFAS concerns, and upcoming capital projects totaling millions of dollars. Marilyn Bend commended the department's work helping Ringle tornado victims.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. He noted the department serves both county and city of Wausau with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after 35 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board recommending waiver of pouts reconnect fees only ($160) for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Forward James Offer and David Tract rezoning approval (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential) to county board
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning approval (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) to county board
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy considerations

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month due to the chair's absence, and approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River. Public comment included objections to the dog day proposal and criticism of Kennedy Park fundraising transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park master plan process from 2023 to present. Discussion clarified that the Friends of Kennedy Park are responsible for fundraising with no set deadline, though the agreement references a 10-year hope for project completion. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital dollars, and staff confirmed no village funds are currently earmarked pending fundraising outcomes. Staff noted the Friends group plans to make a public donation announcement at their June tournament. The committee discussed that the Friends group has raised approximately $85,000 toward a $117,000 goal for field lights.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the location of fallen trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines for equipment access. Dan showed photos of mat installation across TC Energy pipelines. No action was required.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the next meeting because Katrina, who had requested the discussion, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. Public commenter Andy objected to allowing dogs in the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted for the record.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges with removing graffiti from concrete and benches along the trail, noting limited access for pressure washing equipment due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Dan discussed potential solutions including anti-vandalism coatings and seal coats. Committee members suggested volunteer workdays, noting community members love the trail. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan Knopfelski from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version seven of proposed signs for kayak launches along the Eau Claire River. Signs will be installed at launches 3, 4, and 5 in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs include maps, QR codes linking to river gauge data, and historical information specific to each location. Staff noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. A motion to approve installation was made, seconded, and carried unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in discussing food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving a request from a community member. The committee agreed to bring this back for future discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) objected to allowing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking via the name 'Vern M' online, criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned the Kennedy Park funding report showing exactly $8,700 in expenditures as suspicious, and demanded transparency about funds raised including the $10,000 grant, glow event proceeds, and lighting funds, calling the numbers 'convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Defer dog day at aquatic center discussion to June meeting
Approve installation of Eau Claire River kayak launch signs at Weston locations (passed unanimously)
Staff to explore graffiti removal and protective coating options for Eau Claire River Trail
Bring food allergy awareness playground signage discussion to future meeting
Staff to begin Mock Miller Park master plan process with Rettler kickoff meeting
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved minutes and acknowledged budget reports before receiving an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: Jessica from finance reported that TIF 1 closure is anticipated for October/November. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, which are due October 1st. A committee member asked if final numbers still look good, and Jessica confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: The April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report were acknowledged together. Motion by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Passed unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of clerk's office operations. The office has three staff members (two full-time, one part-time), down from four full-time plus two assistants previously. She detailed election administration including Badger Books ($11,000 for four units), noting 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is handled at the clerk's office counter. Pet licensing was brought back in-house from third-party Pet Data, collecting $17,480 through March with $8,067 retained by village. Discussion occurred about newsletter costs ($30,000 annually for printing and postage), with staff noting previous attempts to eliminate mailing were reversed due to resident requests. Chief election inspector wages may be increased from current rate to $15/hour to match county averages.

Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well over the past three Saturdays. Vendors are happy with the setup and location. Full vendor roster with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and some early produce. Committee members raised concerns about parking on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying parking is now allowed on the west side of Alderson while east side remains no parking.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down as this is his last meeting, as he and his wife are moving away from Weston but staying in the metro area. Committee members thanked him for his service.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF 1 bonds, anticipated this summer with bonds due October 1st
Clerk's office to consider increasing chief election inspector pay to $15/hour for next two-year term
Continue seeking part-time clerk's office staff member
Recruit citizens for open committee positions, including this committee and others with vacancies
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee addressed several ordinance changes and enforcement matters, recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, e-bike regulations with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspections, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned structure. A resident raised concerns about the new farmers market location and parking on grass areas.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Roman/Audrey noted some code enforcement cases with suspended enforcement may move back to pending as construction season begins. The property at 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after completing its sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), with 410 traffic stops and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer with recent starts and additional hires coming in May and January. Two SROs work with zoning violations during summer for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded, to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to nuisance ordinances, including tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet) that were switched from a previous meeting. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimpie, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on whether emotos (electric dirt bike-type vehicles) should be allowed on village roadways, sidewalks, and trails. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes that pose safety hazards. The committee debated whether electric scooters used by children should be exempted, with Chief Hunt suggesting officer discretion could handle those situations. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson initiated discussion noting Weston's 12-hour parking restriction window (4am-4pm even/odd) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 4-5 hour overnight windows. DPW Director Michael Wodalski noted the village rarely plows before 4-5am and the overnight restriction frustrates residents. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. The committee discussed potentially matching Rothschild's 2am-7am window and signing specific problem areas. No action taken; Michael Wodalski will identify problem areas and bring recommendations back in July.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school parking lot. Michael Wodalski estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000-3,000, while a lighted system would be $25,000-30,000. Chief Hunt indicated no safety incidents in the area. Committee members expressed reluctance to set precedent for paying for crosswalks on request. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering the installation cost passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment
discussions.body: Fire department proposed joining a regional mobile food vendor inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer. One department's inspection would be valid for all participating municipalities, with an annual sticker system. Chief Hunt noted a recent inspection failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Kimpie, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue sequence issues with an apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight units having separate addresses plus a utility room address needing review. Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval of the address corrections.

Staff requested authorization for a raze order on an abandoned structure that has been posted since 2021 and abandoned since 2010-2014. A planned fire department training burn was cancelled due to unsafe structure conditions. The property owners, who are current on taxes, had previously housed chickens inside the structure. Demolition costs will be added to the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimpie, to recommend board authorization passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, expressing his opinion that it was 'a terrible execution' that would destroy grassy areas. He questioned whether parking vehicles on grass violates village code and suggested ADA accessible parking spaces should be on asphalt rather than grass. He also questioned the origin of proposed fire inspections for mobile food vendors and raised concerns about code enforcement cases that have been pending for extended periods without visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend approval of Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend approval of e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property to Board of Trustees
Michael Wodalski to develop proposal for specific areas requiring even/odd parking restrictions for July meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend board approval of address corrections for E911 compliance
Recommend board authorization to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Investigate property on Sternberg and South Timber for potential code violations
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
